query: 6.4x faster than llama.cpp, 3.9x faster than MLX alternative

results.title: BaseRT - Apple M5 Optimized — AI Assistants — Visalytica
results.content:
BaseRT is emerging as the premier runtime for running large language models (LLMs) on Apple Silicon devices, delivering unprecedented speed and efficiency. Optimized specifically for Apple's M5 chip, it claims to be 6.4 times faster than llama.cpp and 3.9 times faster than MLX, making local AI processing more practical and accessible. Users ranging from developers and AI researchers to hobbyists can install BaseRT with a single command, enabling them to run powerful models directly on their Mac or other Apple devices without relying on cloud infrastructure. Its focus on open-source technology and performance optimization makes it particularly appealing for those seeking to leverage AI locally while maximizing hardware capabilities. The tool's ease of use combined with its speed advantages [...] BaseRT - Apple M5 Optimized

# BaseRT - Apple M5 Optimized

6.4x faster than llama.cpp, 3.9x faster than MLX

## About BaseRT - Apple M5 Optimized

results.title: BaseRT Review - AI Tool Features and Tips
results.content:
Blazing Speed: Up to 6.4x faster than llama.cpp and 3.9x faster than MLX on prefill, and significant gains on decode.
 One-Command Installation: Get up and running instantly with `curl -LsSf  | sh`.
 Offline Operation & Privacy: Run models entirely on your device, ensuring no data leaves your machine and no API keys are needed.
 Coding Agent Integration: Seamlessly serve models for local coding agents, keeping your entire development workflow private and on-device.
 Broad Model Support: From Qwen to Llama, Gemma, Mistral, Phi-3, and Nomic BERT, a wide array of popular open-source models are supported.

## Who’s BaseRT For? Empowering On-Device AI Engineers [...] Visit Website  Have an AI Tool? Submit it here!

The benchmarks speak for themselves. BaseRT isn’t just a marginal improvement; it’s a paradigm shift in local LLM execution. It achieves staggering speedups against industry standards, turning your Apple Silicon machine into a formidable AI powerhouse.

|  |  |
 --- |
| Benchmark (Tokens/sec) | BaseRT vs. Competitors |
| Prefill: Qwen3 30B-A3B (pp128) | 2,478 (+94% vs MLX, 6.4x vs llama.cpp) |
| Decode: Qwen3 0.6B (tg128Q4) | 531 (+33% vs MLX, +38% vs llama.cpp) |
| Prefill: Gemma 4 E2B (pp2048) | 16,264 (+32% vs MLX, 6.4x vs llama.cpp) |

## Core Capabilities: Unleashing Local LLMs

results.title: Llama.cpp vs MLX on Apple Mx
results.content:
The Groundy benchmarks make this concrete. On an M1 Max running Qwen3.5–35B at 8.5K context, MLX reported 51 tokens per second in the UI but the effective wall-clock throughput including prefill collapsed to about 3 tokens per second. llama.cpp with `--flash-attn` finished the same response 9 seconds faster (43.4 seconds versus 52.3 seconds). At long contexts, the prefill gap is real.

This is the part of the comparison that resists the clean narrative. MLX has a fused attention kernel, but llama.cpp has Flash Attention proper, and at long contexts that matters. The community is closing the gap (see TurboQuant-MLX, Open-TQ-Metal), but as of today, llama.cpp with `--flash-attn` is faster on long contexts than MLX out of the box.

## Where each one wins [...] Apple Silicon performance for the common case. For 4-bit quantized models in the 7B to 30B range, MLX is typically faster than llama.cpp on the same hardware. The gap widens with the M5 Neural Accelerators, where Apple’s recent benchmarks show 3x speedups over M4 on certain workloads.
 fine-tuning. LoRA, QLoRA, and DoRA are in the box. llama.cpp is inference-only. If you are doing parameter-efficient fine-tuning on a Mac Studio, MLX is the only serious option.
 Swift integration. mlx-swift makes native macOS and iOS apps with local inference straightforward in a way that no llama.cpp wrapper does. For client work where the deliverable is a native macOS application, this is decisive.

results.title: Apple MLX vs llama.cpp: Which is Really Faster? (4 Runtimes - Ollama Included)
results.content: Unlike llama.cpp, MLX compiles metal kernels lazily. So, there's a certain warm-up period until performance stabilizes. Though, it's just a theory. We'll need more digging. So, what did we get? On the left, you can see filtered cold prompt half context runs. On the right, full context ones. To avoid measuring this warm-up effect, I feel it's fair to take the fastest numbers for comparison. And do that for all the previous run times, too. The pre-fill speed of these three runs are almost as fast as MLX LM. And the right side, we can only compare to llama.cpp because other run times failed. So, let's gather all the results and see the conclusions. Llama.cpp is the baseline. At 49k context, it does 584 tokens per second for pre-fill and 32 tokens for generation. While LM Studio MLX [...] pre-fill and 32 tokens for generation. While LM Studio MLX implementation gave a very similar pre-fill speed to llama.cpp, but its generation performance was higher of them all, which is a bit surprising. Like plus 51% surprising. But, I couldn't use it for the full context test because of swapping kicking in. On the other hand, running MLX LM directly turned out to be the most impressive for pre-fill at 745 tokens per second. It left all the run times behind, but its caching wasn't working consistently and it crashed my Mac with a bigger context. And here we come to a llama with the latest MLX implementation. Its pre-fill is 19% faster than llama.cpp at 49k context. And a whopping 34% faster at 95. But, these are its best-case numbers. And I wish it was less fluctuating in performance, [...] you're curious. Want to try Rotor Quant? Here's a fork of a fork of the Turbo Quant and a video, too. You get the idea. It's very active, customizable, and you can tune the runtime to your heart's content. By the way, if you're enjoying this, leave a like and subscribe. But there are some wild claims floating around that MLX turns out way faster than llama.cpp backed runtimes. All llama block claims that the recently added MLX engine increases pre-fill speeds by more than 50% and almost twice for generation. This sounds incredible and we will check that. But then there's this MLX wrapper wrapper which claims being 224 times faster than LM Studio. Wait. Is there a small comma somewhere here? 2 2 4 Nope. Uh the times of throwing numbers at the wall. You can't compare cash pre-fill against
